title: Fed Holds Rates Steady at 4.25-4.50%, Signals Two Cuts in 2026
text:
The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) voted unanimously on Wednesday to maintain the federal funds rate at its target range of 4.25% to 4.50%, in line with market expectations. The decision follows two consecutive 25-basis-point cuts in September and November 2025, which brought rates down from the peak of 5.25-5.50% maintained for over a year.

In the accompanying statement, the Committee acknowledged that 'economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace' while noting that 'inflation remains somewhat elevated relative to the Committee's 2 percent goal.' The statement removed previous language about 'further progress' on inflation, signaling a more cautious approach to additional rate reductions.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, in his press conference following the decision, emphasized the central bank's data-dependent approach. 'We do not need to be in a hurry to adjust our policy stance,' Powell said. 'The economy is strong, the labor market is solid, and inflation is moving gradually toward our 2% target. We can afford to be patient.'

The updated Summary of Economic Projections, or 'dot plot,' showed the median FOMC participant expects two 25-basis-point cuts in 2026, down from three cuts projected at the December 2025 meeting. The median projection for the federal funds rate at end-2026 is 3.75-4.00%, implying cuts at the June and September meetings if the baseline scenario holds.

Key economic projections were revised as follows: GDP growth for 2026 was upgraded to 2.3% from 2.0%, reflecting stronger-than-expected consumer spending. Core PCE inflation for 2026 was revised slightly higher to 2.5% from 2.3%, reflecting sticky services inflation. The unemployment rate projection was maintained at 4.1% for 2026.

Market reaction was muted, as the decision was largely priced in. Treasury yields edged higher, with the 10-year yield rising 4 basis points to 4.32%. The S&P 500 closed roughly flat, while the dollar index (DXY) strengthened marginally. Fed funds futures markets are pricing in a 68% probability of a June cut, with a total of 45 basis points of easing expected by year-end 2026.

Economists have flagged several risks to the Fed's base case. Potential tariff increases following recent trade policy announcements could reignite inflation pressures, while any deterioration in labor market conditions could accelerate the pace of cuts. 'The Fed is walking a fine line between supporting growth and ensuring inflation is durably on a path back to 2%,' noted Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. 'Their patience is appropriate given the uncertainty, but they may need to pivot quickly if conditions change.'

The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for May 6-7, 2026, with the market consensus expecting rates to remain on hold pending further clarity on inflation data and labor market trends.
